body_txt: The end of both the Pleistocene Epoch and the last Glacial Maximum (commonly referred to as the Last Ice Age) occurred around 10,000 years ago, which also saw the gradual extinction on most continents (most of Africa and parts of Asia kept most of their megafauna) of most of the megafauna that dominated the Pleistocene world, such as mammoths, mastodons , ground sloths, glyptodonts, and saber-toothed cats . The causes of the extinction of the megafauna are heavily debated, with some scientists pinning the blame on the warming climate of the time, and others pointing the finger at overhunting by a certain little bipedal ape named Homo sapiens . Others still believe that it was a combination of both those things, plus other factors like disease playing a role. Note, that a now-debunked hypothesis even involved a meteorite impact . Works set during the end of the Pleistocene typically show the climate getting warmer, with the glaciers retreating. Expect to see the ancient megafauna gradually declining as their environment changes around them, their food sources disappear, and they become increasingly desperate to survive. And, of course, expect to see human hunters preying on the already struggling giant animals. Another theme of this time involves the last of the giant predators, such as saber-tooths, dire wolves, and short-faced bears, finding their large prey going extinct and the remaining animals too fast to be caught and/or too small to sustain the large, specialised predators, or being forced to compete with each other for what little prey is left. It should be noted that not all extinct megafauna died out 10,000 years ago. For example, a population of woolly mammoths on Wrangel Island in Russia survived until about 4,000 years ago, meaning that they were living out their last days during the construction of the pyramids in Egypt. Moas in New Zealand managed to survive until the 17th Century, and some of the smaller species may have made it into the 19th. Further, most of the famous megafauna of Australia (giant monitor lizards, marsupial lions, giant kangaroos and wombats) became extinct around 40,000 years ago, i.e. still around the end of the Pleistocene but substantially earlier. It should also be noted that this trope refers to the ending of the last Glacial Maximum that ended the Pleistocene, not the ends of the other Glacial Maximums that preceded it. It should also be noted that it is becoming increasingly untenable to believe that climate changes alone caused the extinctions since the megafauna had survived both cold and warm periods before with a good number of species even preferring warmer climates. The smaller species that “replaced” the bigger megafauna had actually coexisted with the megafauna for many thousands of years at least, and were likely spared from the wave of extinctions for various reasons. It has been speculated that the myths surrounding The Great Flood in several cultures were inspired by the glaciers melting and the sea levels rising at the end of the last Ice Age. An example of End of an Age . Compare The Day the Dinosaurs Died . For tropes about specific Ice Age megafauna, see Mammoths Mean Ice Age and Snowy Sabertooths .
